perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient agrees that, for eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, no person in its organization who receives or has access to the Evaluation Material or has knowledge about the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit, or cause the soliciting of, or assist or encourage others in the soliciting of, the employment or consulting services of any of the officers of a Provider or any of its subsidiaries or employees engaged in research and development for Provider or any of its subsidiaries or any other employee of the Provider or any if its subsidiaries with whom the Recipient has had contact in connection with its evaluation of a Possible Transaction or of whom it has become aware as a result of its receipt of any Evaluation Material, so long as they are employed by the Provider or any of its subsidiaries and for two months thereafter (each, a “ Covered Employee ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient agrees that, for eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, no person in its organization who receives or has access to the Evaluation Material or has knowledge about the Possible Transaction will, directly or indirectly, solicit, or cause the soliciting of, or assist or encourage others in the soliciting of, the employment or consulting services of any of the officers of a Provider or any of its subsidiaries or employees engaged in research and development for Provider or any of its subsidiaries or any other employee of the Provider or any if its subsidiaries with whom the Recipient has had contact in connection with its evaluation of a Possible Transaction or of whom it has become aware as a result of its receipt of any Evaluation Material, so long as they are employed by the Provider or any of its subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the two-month restriction after employment ends, the agreement implies that solicitation is permissible immediately after an employee leaves the Provider. This weakens protection against unfair competition, especially if the departing employees are privy to sensitive information obtained through this agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While the original clause has a time limit, its removal makes the restriction overly broad and may be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade if it prevents former employees from being solicited for new opportunities immediately after leaving their former employer.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the two-month buffer after an employee leaves the Provider, potentially conflicting with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The original text protected against solicitation for a limited time post-employment, whereas the modified text implies immediate solicitation is permissible. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," with the caveat that this section should be read broadly. Removing the time restriction could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly if it prevents former employees from being solicited for new opportunities immediately after leaving their former employer.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient agrees not to use any Evaluation Material of the Provider in violation of applicable securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient agrees to use Evaluation Material of the Provider in accordance with their own interpretations.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the explicit requirement to comply with securities laws, implying that the Recipient can use the information however they see fit, regardless of legal consequences. This introduces legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. By stating the Recipient can interpret the use as they see fit, this implies it is acceptable to use inside information (Evaluation Material) for trading which violates this law.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures compliance with securities laws, while the changed text allows the recipient to use Evaluation Material based on their own interpretations, removing the explicit legal obligation. This directly contradicts Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The scraped law snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 78j, addresses manipulative and deceptive devices, and indicates that using inside information for trading, as implied in the changed text, would be a violation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party understands and agrees that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by either Party or any of its Representatives and that the Party against which such breach is committed shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach or threat thereof. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by either Party of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the Party against which such breach is committed.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party understands and agrees that money damages may be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by either Party or any of its Representatives and that the Party against which such breach is committed shall be limited to monetary compensation as a remedy for any such breach or threat thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting remedies to only monetary compensation, it removes the possibility of seeking equitable relief like injunctions or specific performance. In cases of trade secret misappropriation, this could be insufficient, as stopping the misuse of confidential information is often critical.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By limiting the remedies to only monetary damages, the modified text prevents a party from obtaining an injunction to stop the misuse of trade secrets, contradicting the CUTSA's provision for injunctive relief.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code Section 3426.2(a) of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The original text allows for equitable relief, including injunctions, for breaches. The changed text limits remedies to monetary compensation, removing the possibility of injunctive relief. Snippet 1, CIV 3426.2 (a), explicitly states, "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By restricting remedies to only monetary damages, the modified contract prevents a party from seeking an injunction to stop the misuse of trade secrets, directly contradicting the statute's provision for injunctive relief, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Party and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of California applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is for the benefit of each Party and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of [Company X's Choice] without regard to any conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law away from California law and replacing it with '[Company X's Choice]' and adding 'without regard to any conflict of law principles' introduces legal risk. A different choice of law clause could reduce consumer protections.
perturbation.law_citation: California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 1750
perturbation.law_explanation: California provides strong consumer protection laws. Moving the governing law outside of California would likely invalidate some of these protections. For example, The Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) protects consumers from unfair or deceptive business practices. Choosing a different state's law could deny California residents these protections. Additionally, the phrase 'without regard to any conflict of law principles' explicitly prevents a court from considering California law even if it should apply under conflict of law rules.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1750 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces California law with '[Company X's Choice]' and adds 'without regard to any conflict of law principles'. This creates a legal contradiction by potentially removing consumer protections mandated by California law. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the legal risk of reduced consumer protections. The Law Explanation specifies that moving the governing law outside of California would likely invalidate some consumer protections, such as those provided by the Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA). Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies CIV 1750 as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act, confirming its existence within California law. By choosing a different state's law, California residents could be denied these protections, which contradicts the intent and application of California's consumer protection laws. The original text ensures compliance with California law, while the changed text actively avoids it.
